coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent silicon nanoparticles for sensing sulfide ions and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Chao
Li, Ting
Yang, Qin
Guo, Yongming
Tao, Tao
coredata.dc:description: We have presented a hydrothermal approach for synthesizing fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (F-SiNPs) with yellow-green emission. The obtained F-SiNPs exhibited excellent stability and good biocompatibility. By virtue of the specific reaction between S2- and 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB), colorimetric assay of S2- was realized with a good linear range of 0–100 μM. The colorimetric detection system could be further combined with F-SiNPs to construct a probe for fluorescence turn-off sensing S2- in aqueous solution due to inner filter effect. In the fluorescent detection system, a good linearity with S2- concentration in the range of 0–50 μM was accomplished. And as low as 0.1 μM S2- was successfully detected. Moreover, the F-SiNPs displayed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, and was further utilized for cell imaging. These results demonstrated the promising applications of F-SiNPs in S2- analysis and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent silicon nanoparticles
Sulfide ions
Detection
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of quantum yield of F-SiNPs
Colorimetric detection of S2- with DTNB
Fluorescent detection of S2- with F-SiNPs
Detection of S2- in real samples with F-SiNPs
In vitro cytotoxicity test of F-SiNPs
pH effects on the fluorescence intensity of F-SiNPs
Colorimetric detection of S2- with DTNB
Fluorescent detection of S2- with F-SiNPs
Determination of S2- in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation of F-SiNPs and the applications for sensing S2- and cell imaging.
(A) Fluorescence excitation spectrum of F-SiNPs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of F-SiNPs at different excitation wavelengths.
(A) XPS Survey spectrum of F-SiNPs. (B) FT-IR spectrum of F-SiNPs.

(A) The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of F-SiNPs (λex = 430 nm). (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of S2-, DTNB, and S2-+DTNB (Conditions: 50 mM pH = 9.0 borax-borate buffer, [DTNB] = 100 μM, [S2-] = 100 μM, [EDTA] = 0.5 mM).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of DTNB in the presence of different concentration of S2-. (B) Plot of A/A0 versus of the concentration of S2-, inset: the linear section of the plot of A/A0 versus of the concentration of S2-. (C) Correspondingly photographs of DTNB in the presence of different concentrations of S2- (Conditions: 50 mM pH = 9.0 borax-borate buffer, [DTNB] = 100 μM, [EDTA] = 0.5 mM).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of F-SiNPs-DTNB system in the presence of different concentration of S2- (λex = 430 nm). (B) Plot of I/I0 versus of the concentration of S2-, inset: the linear section of the plot of I/I0 versus of the concentration of S2- (Conditions: 50 mM pH = 9.0 borax-borate buffer, [DTNB] = 100 μM, [EDTA] = 0.5 mM).

(A) Normalized UV–vis absorbance spectrum of DTNB and S2- (black), and normalized fluorescence excitation (red) and emission spectra of F-SiNPs (green). (B) Selectivity of the F-SiNPs-DTNB system for fluorescent detection of S2- over other potential interferences with the concentration of 200 μM, I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of the detection system in the absence and presence of S2- or other potential interferences, respectively. Black and red bars represent I/I0 in the absence and presence of S2-, respectively (Conditions: 50 mM pH = 9.0 borax-borate buffer, [DTNB] = 100 μM, [EDTA] = 0.5 mM). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Confocal fluorescence, (B) bright-field, and (C) overlay images of HeLa cells incubated with F-SiNPs. The fluorescence emission was collected from 480 to 600 nm intensities upon excited at 448 nm.

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent silicon nanoparticles for sensing sulfide ions and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology, Nanjing 210044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science
Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology, Nanjing 210044, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have presented a hydrothermal approach for synthesizing fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (F-SiNPs) with yellow-green emission. The obtained F-SiNPs exhibited excellent stability and good biocompatibility. By virtue of the specific reaction between S2- and 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB), colorimetric assay of S2- was realized with a good linear range of 0–100 μM. The colorimetric detection system could be further combined with F-SiNPs to construct a probe for fluorescence turn-off sensing S2- in aqueous solution due to inner filter effect. In the fluorescent detection system, a good linearity with S2- concentration in the range of 0–50 μM was accomplished. And as low as 0.1 μM S2- was successfully detected. Moreover, the F-SiNPs displayed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, and was further utilized for cell imaging. These results demonstrated the promising applications of F-SiNPs in S2- analysis and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials have received much interest due to their tunable fluorescence emission, ease of fabrication, good biocompatibility and excellent photostability, which make them to be applied in various fields, including environment, chemistry and biology [1–5]. However, the traditional heavy metal-based quantum dots (QDs), such as CdTe QDs, CdS QDs, cannot avoid the toxicity of these heavy metal ions [5,6]. And the newly developed gold and silver nanoclusters faced with the problems of high price of these noble metals [7,8]. Therefore, considerable efforts are highly desirable to develop new type fluorescent nanomaterials.

Recently, fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (F-SiNPs) have attracted much interest due to their excellent water-solubility, good biocompatibility and ease of fabrication [9]. As a result, considerable efforts have been devoted to developing lots of strategies for fabrication a variety of F-SiNPs [9,10]. Chen’s group presented a simple approach for fabrication of F-SiNPs with green emission by utilizing 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane as a silicon source and L-ascorbic acid as the reduction reagent at 40 °C. The resultant F-SiNPs was successfully used for fluorescence “turn-off” assay of heparin owing to the aggregation of F-SiNPs induced by the hydrogen bonding between F-SiNPs and heparin [11]. And green emissive F-SiNPs were also prepared via hydrothermal treatment of N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]- ethylenediamine and catechol. The obtained F-SiNPs were fabricated a fluorescence turn-off nanosensor for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol with a wide linear range [12]. Yuan and coworkers presented a hydrothermal approach for synthesizing blue emitting F-SiNPs using disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate and 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane as sources. The resulted F-SiNPs was utilized for fluorescence turn-off detection of catechol and hydroquinone [13]. Wang’s group has prepared F-SiNPs with green emission through the thermal treatment of N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine and m-phenylenediamine. The obtained F-SiNPs were employed for fluorescence turn-off assay of nitrite [14]. Also, yellow-green emitting F-SiNPs produced from hydrothermal treatment of 4-aminophenol and N-aminoethyl-γ-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane were found to be sensitive to pH, and were further used for cell imaging [15]. These reports indicated that F-SiNPs could be constructed into various fluorescent nanosensors.

Sulfide ion (S2-) is a pollutant that is commonly found in industrial wastewater. It exhibited dangerous effects on human health [16,17]. It is, thus, of essence to construct selective and sensitive sensors for assay of S2-. Hemmateenejad’s group designed a fluorescence turn-off assay of S2- by using tannic acid-stabilized copper nanoclusters (CuNCs)/Cu2+ system. The formation of CuS could effectively quench the fluorescence of the CuNCs due to the inner filter effect (IFE) [18]. Wang et al. presented an “on–off-on” fluorescent sensor for sensing S2- based on the fact that S2- could recover the fluorescence of g-C3N4 nanosheets quenched by Ag+ [19]. Zhang et al. have synthesized silk fibroin protected fluorescent CuNCs (SF-CuNCs) for fluorescence turn-on assay of S2- according to the fluorescence enhancement caused by S2--induced aggregation of SF-CuNCs to large nanoparticles [20]. However, the aggregates of SF-CuNCs were unstable, which may affect the accuracy of fluorescence signal. And coumarin@guanosine monophosphate-Tb coordinated polymer was also designed for ratiometric fluorescence detection of S2- based on the specific interaction between S2- and Fe2+ [21]. Also, Ding’s group proposed a simple strategy for preparing bovine serum albumin-erbium(III) complex-modulated gold-silver bimetallic nanoclusters (BSA-Er/Au/AgNCs) with dual emissions at 400 nm and 585 nm. The as-prepared BSA-Er/Au/AgNCs was used for ratiometric fluorescence assay of S2- because of the generation of Ag2S layer on Au/AgNCs [22]. The work presents a simple approach for fabrication of fluorescent nanosensors. And silvernanoclusterscapped silicananoparticles(SiO2@AgNCs) have been established for ratiometric fluorescence detection of S2- and I-. The [Ru(bpy)3]2+ in silica nanoparticles exhibited the fluorescence at 602 nm served as the reference signal, and the in-situ formation of AgNCs emitted the fluorescence at 500 nm acted as the responsive signal for sensing S2- and I-. Based on the ratiometric fluorescence change, sensitive detection of S2- and I- was accomplished [23]. However, the selectivity for S2- detection should be improved. These cases provide excellent approaches for sensing S2-. Nevertheless, most reports involved in the use of environmentally unfriendly reagents or complicated fabrication procedures, and there are rarely reports on the use of F-SiNPs with biocompatibility to determine S2-. Thus, F-SiNPs are needed to be exploited for S2- assay.

Herein, we presented a hydrothermal strategy for preparation of F-SiNPs by using 2-hydroxyphenylboronic acid and 3-(2-aminoethylamino) propyltriethoxysilane) as sources. The obtained F-SiNPs exhibited yellow-green emission, good photostability and excellent salt tolerance. By using the specific reaction between 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) and S2-, colorimetric assay of S2- was accomplished with a good linear range of 0–100 μM. The colorimetric detection system was further combined with F-SiNPs to construct a probe for fluorescence turn-off assay of S2- in aqueous solution owing to IFE with a linear range of 0–50 μM. And as low as 0.1 μM S2- was successfully detected. Moreover, the F-SiNPs exhibited low cytotoxicity, and was further utilized for cell imaging (Fig. 1 ). These results suggested the promising applications of F-SiNPs in S2- analysis and bioimaging.

2-Hydroxyphenylboronic acid, 3-(2-aminoethylamino)propyltriethoxysilane), Na2S·9H2O, 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Inc. Other reagents were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification. Ultrapure water (>18.2 MΩ•cm) was used for all the experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were collected on a Cary Eclipse luminescence spectrometer (Varian, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained from a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-3600, Japan) with a 1 cm path length cuvette. A Nicolet 5700 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo-Electron Corp., USA) was utilized to identify the functional groups of F-SiNPs with the KBr pellet technique. Fluorescence lifetime was measured from a FLS 980 spectrofluorimeter (Edinburgh Instrument, UK) under the excitation of 430 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific). The morphology feature of F-SiNPs was performed on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Photostability tests were performed at room temperature using the 365 nm light from an ultraviolet lamp (16 W, CBIO-UV3A, Beijing CBIO Bioscience Technology Co. Ltd., Beijing, China). And photographs were obtained using a Canon 700D digital camera.

One-pot hydrothermal approach was used for the synthesis of F-SiNPs according to the preciously reported method with slight modification [24]. Briefly, 15 mg of 2-hydroxyphenylboronic acid was firstly dissolved in 8 mL of ultrapure water. And 2 mL of 3-(2-aminoethylamino) propyltriethoxysilane) was added into the solution. After stirring for about 10 min, the mixed solution was transferred into a 20 mL of Teflon-line stainless steel autoclave and incubated under 180 °C for 4 h (CAUTION: High temperature). After cooling to room temperature, yellow solution was obtained. After purification through dialysis with dialysis tube (MWCO = 500 Da) for 6 h, the purified F-SiNPs were collected and stored under 4 °C for further use. Moreover, the concentration of the F-SiNPs was calculated to be approximately 17 mg/mL by using weighing method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of quantum yield of F-SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The relative quantum yield (QY) of the F-SiNPs was determined based on the previous established method [25,26]. The absorbance at 430 nm excitation wavelength was maintained below 0.1 to minimize self-absorption. The QY of F-SiNPs was measured via a comparative method using rhodamine 6G (QY = 95%, in ethanol) as the reference solution with excitation wavelength at 430 nm and calculated on the basis of the following equation:
Q
Y
F - S i N P s
= Q
Y
re
A
F - S i N P s
/
A
re
n
F - S i N P s
/
n
re

where A is the slope calculated from the curves of the integral area of fluorescence emission spectra of F-SiNPs and rhodamine 6G under the excitation of 430 nm versus the corresponding absorbance, and n is the refractive index of solvent. The subscripts “F-SiNPs” and “re” refer to the F-SiNPs and reference, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of S2- with DTNB
ce:section.ce:para: The colorimetric detection of S2- was conducted in pH = 9.0 borax-borate buffer solution (pH 9.0, 40 mM). In the typical detection process, borax-borate buffer solution (600 μL), EDTA (15 μL, 100 mM), DTNB (60 μL, 5 mM) and different concentrations of Na2S were mixed together. Furthermore, ultrapure water was required to dilute the mixture into 3.0 mL. After incubation for 1 min at room temperature, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixture were recorded, and the corresponding pictures were also recorded using the camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of S2- with F-SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent detection of S2- was similar to that of the colorimetric detection. Namely, borax-borate buffer solution (600 μL), EDTA (15 μL, 100 mM), DTNB (60 μL, 5 mM) and different concentrations of Na2S were mixed together. After incubation for 1 min at room temperature, 30 μL of F-SiNPs solution was added the above mixture. And ultrapure water was required to dilute the mixture into 3.0 mL and mixed thoroughly. The mixture was further incubated for 10 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture were recorded under excitation of 430 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S2- in real samples with F-SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Lake and tap water were collected in the campus and laboratory, respectively. The real water samples were firstly filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to get rid of any suspended particles in water. The pH values of tap water and lake water were about 7–8 and 6–7, respectively. Then, 50 μL water sample, 120 μL S2- (1 mM), 600 μL borax-borate buffer solution, EDTA (15 μL, 100 mM), DTNB (60 μL, 5 mM) were mixed together and incubation for 1 min. Subsequently, 30 μL of F-SiNPs solution was added the above mixture. And ultrapure water was used to dilute the mixture into 3.0 mL. The mixture was mixed thoroughly and further incubated for 10 min at room temperature. Afterwards, the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture were collected under excitation of 430 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity test of F-SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro cytotoxicity of F-SiNPs was evaluated with 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assy. HeLa cells were firstly seeded in 96-well plate with a density of 1 × 104 cells/well and then incubated at 37 °C with a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere for 24 h. After the cells attachment, the F-SiNPs solution with a series of different concentrations ranging from 0 to 1700 mg/L were added into the cells and cultured with HeLa cells for 24 h. The viability of HeLa cells in the culture environment of F-SiNPs was then analyzed with MTT cytotoxicity assay kit.

HeLa cells were firstly seeded in a 35 mm glass-bottom dish with a density of 3 × 105 cells/dish and then incubated at 37 °C with a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere for 24 h. After cells attachment, the F-SiNPs solution was added into the cells and incubated for 4 h. Subsequently, the cells were washed with phosphate buffer saline for three times, and then photographed with confocal microscope. The cells were excited at 448 nm and the emission was collected from 480 nm to 600 nm.

The morphological structures of the F-SiNPs were characterized by TEM, the F-SiNPs were quasi-spherical, and the average size was about 2.3 nm ( Figure S7 ). To further indentify the elemental compositions and surface functional groups of F-SiNPs. The elemental ingredients of F-SiNPs were evaluated by using XPS. Four main peaks attributed to Si1s, C1s, N1s and O1s were easily noticed, demonstrating that the F-SiNPs were composed of Si, C, N and O (Fig. 3
A). Surprisingly, B was not observed in the XPS spectrum. And repeated tests were conducted in different labs, B was still not found ( Figure S8 ). The reason may be attributed to the electron-deficient property and low content of B. The electron-deficient property makes B difficult to emit photoelectrons. And the low content further causes B difficult to be detected by XPS. And the C1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.5 eV, 286.7 eV and 288.8 eV ( Figure S9 A), which were corresponding to C-Si/C-C/C = C/C-B, C-N, C-O and C-NHx (x = 1,2) [29–33]. The spectrum of N1s was divided into three peaks at 399.9 eV, 400.6 eV and 402.4 eV ( Figure S9 B), which could be recognized as C = N-C, N-H and N-(C)3 [14,34]. The spectrum of O1s was separated into two peaks at 532.2 eV and 531.4 eV, which could be signified as O-C and O-Si ( Figure S9 C) [31]. And the Si2p spectrum was separated into two peaks at 101.5 eV (S-C) and 102.0 eV (Si-O) ( Figure S9 D) [14,35]. In the FT-IR spectrum, the peak at 3420 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H [36]. The C-H stretching vibrations displayed at 2927 cm−1 [37]. And the strong peak at 1655 cm−1 could be ascribed to the bending vibrations of the aromatic C = C groups [38]. The signal at about 1260 cm−1 could be assigned to the C-O stretching vibration. The two absorption peaks at about 1080 cm−1 and 1348 cm−1 could be corresponding to the C-B and B-O stretching vibrations [39]. The peaks at around 1020 cm−1 and 1080 cm−1 could be attributed to the Si-O bending vibration [30]. And the peak at about 795 cm−1 might originate from the C-H deformation of aromatics (Fig. 3 B) [40]. These results demonstrated that the F-SiNPs have been functionalized by abundant N-H, O-H, C-B, C-O, C-H and other functional groups, ensuring the excellent water solubility, good optical properties and remarkable sensing performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effects on the fluorescence intensity of F-SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
We further studied the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of F-SiNPs. In pH of 1, the fluorescence intensity was the lowest. And fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the increase of pH from 2 to 5. However, the fluorescence intensity showed a gradual rise trend in pH from 6 to 9, and a decline in pH from 10 to 13. And the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum extent at pH 9.0 (Fig. 4
A). Therefore, we chose pH of 9 as the optimal detection condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of S2- with DTNB
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, we studied the response of DTNB to S2- by using UV–vis absorption spectra. The DTNB solution displayed a peak at about 323 nm, and no characteristic absorption peak was found in the S2- solution. However, after the addition of S2- to the solution of DTNB, a new peak centered at about 408 nm was easily observed (Fig. 4 B), which was ascribed to the formation of 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoate (TNB) resulted from the reaction between S2- and DTNB (Fig. 1) [31,41,42]. Because of the resulted TNB exhibited yellow, the colorimetric assay of S2- was easily achieved.Before colorimetric detection of S2-, the incubation time was optimized. As depicted in Figure S10 , the effect of incubation time on the absorbance changes of DTNB solution at 408 nm varying from 0 to 10 min in the presence of S2- was demonstrated. The addition of S2- caused the absorbance rapidly increase to the maximum, and the absorbance remained unchanged even after 10 min ( Figure S10 ). Thus, 1 min was conservatively selected as the best incubation time for the reaction between DTNB and S2-.

Under the optimal conditions, the colorimetric determination of S2- with DTNB was demonstrated. As shown in Fig. 5
A, the addition of S2- led to the gradual increase of the peak at 408 nm and concomitant decline of the peak at 324 nm. When the concentration of S2- increased to 200 μM, the absorbance at 408 nm no further increased obviously. These results suggested that DTNB could be employed for colorimetric assay of S2-. The absorbance changes at 408 nm (A/A0) exhibited good linearity with the S2- concentration ranging from 0 to 100 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.998 (Fig. 5 B). And the lowest detection concentration of S2- was 0.1 μM. At the same time, the color of the DTNB solution gradually changed to yellow, the lowest detection concentration of S2- with the naked eyes was 2.5 μM (Fig. 5 C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of S2- with F-SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
With increasing the concentrations of S2- in the range of 0–200 μM, the fluorescence intensity of F-SiNPs gradually declined at the fluorescence emission wavelength of 515 nm. When the concentration of S2- reached 200 μM, the fluorescence intensity at 515 nm did not further decreased obviously (Fig. 6
A). And the degree of fluorescence intensity quenching was 85.6% when the S2- concentration was 100 μM, revealing that S2- could effectively quench the fluorescence of F-SiNPs. A graph about the changes of fluorescence intensity at 515 nm and the concentrations of S2- in the range of 0–200 μM was made. Also an excellent linear correlation between the changes of fluorescence intensity at 515 nm and the S2- concentrations from 0 to 50 μM was achieved with correlation coefficient of 0.994 (Fig. 6 B). And the lowest detection concentration of S2- was 0.1 μM. Compared to other reported optical methods, the proposed fluorescence approach exhibited comparable sensitivity for quantification of S2- (Table S1) [20,43–50]. However, the developed sensing system did not require metal salts, complicated operations. In short, the developed fluorescence method for sensing S2- displayed many merits of ease of fabrication, low cost and rapid response, which could be further utilized for detection of S2- in real samples.

We further evaluated the detection mechanism of F-SiNPs and DTNB for fluorescence sensing S2- by using UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence decay technique. There are mainly three fluorescence quenching mechanisms: IFE, fluorescence resonance energy transfer and photoinduced electron transfer mechanism. As previously discussed, the reaction between S2- and DTNB could yield yellow TNB with a maximum absorption peak at 408 nm. The absorption spectrum of TNB mostly overlapped with the excitation spectrum of F-SiNPs (Fig. 7
A), suggesting that IFE might contribute to the fluorescence quenching because the produced yellow TNB could adsorb the excitation light [31,42]. And the fluorescence emission spectrum of F-SiNPs did not overlap with the absorption spectrum of TNB, indicating that fluorescence resonance energy transfer was not found to quenching the fluorescence of F-SiNPs. The fluorescence decay technique was further utilized to elucidate the detection mechanism. It was easily observed that the addition of DTNB and S2- did not obviously alter the fluorescence lifetime ( Figure S13 ), further ruling out the contribution of photoinduced electron transfer mechanism [25,51–53]. Therefore, IFE was the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the detection system.

The selectivity is also of great significance for a detection system. To evaluate the selectivity of the F-SiNPs towards S2-, the influences of various possible interfering substances such as Cl-, Br-, I-, SO4 2-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, Ac-, H2PO4 -, P2O7 2-, Ca2+ and Mg2+ on the fluorescence intensity of F-SiNPs were demonstrated. And the concentration of these interferences was 200 μM. As expected, these interferences exhibited negligible impacts, and only S2- caused an evident increase in fluorescence intensity at 515 nm. Furthermore, the anti-interference capability of the detection system was also demonstrated by mixing S2- with these interferences, and no significant effect on fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 7 B). Although some possible interfering species, such as biothiols, that can react with DTNB to produce TNB, may disturb the detection of S2-, these species are rarely observed in waste water because they are easily oxidized or coordinated with other metal ions [54–56]. Furthermore, the detection is more suitable for the assay of S2- in the real samples without sulfhydryl group-containing compounds. Notably, the EDTA in borax-borate buffer can mask the potential interfering metal ions that may affect the fluorescence of F-SiNPs, further improving the selectivity. The excellent selectivity is due to the production of yellow-colored TNB from the specific reaction between S2- and DTNB. These results revealed that the proposed method had high selectivity for S2- determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of S2- in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity is important for the biological applications of fluorescent nanomaterials. And MTT assay was applied for evaluating the cytotoxicity of F-SiNPs. As exhibited Figure S15 the cell viability values of HeLa cells exceeded 91% after 24 h when the concentration of F-SiNPs reached to 1700 mg/L, implying that F-SiNPs displayed low cytotoxic impact on HeLa cells. And we further used HeLa cells as model cells to investigate the bioimaging applications of F-SiNPs. The cellular uptakes of F-SiNPs were observed via confocal microscopy. As shown in Fig. 8 , red fluorescence was observed inside the cells, suggesting that the F-SiNPs were internalized by the HeLa cells. And most cells maintained normal morphology, indicating that F-SiNPs are biocompatible and have low cytotoxicity to cells. These results demonstrated that the F-SiNPs possessed good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and could be utilized for cell imaging.

To conclude, we have presented a hydrothermal approach for synthesizing F-SiNPs with yellow-green emission. The obtained F-SiNPs exhibited excellent stability and good biocompatibility. By virtue of the specific reaction between S2- and DTNB, colorimetric assay of S2- was realized with a good linear range of 0–100 μM. The colorimetric detection system was further combined with F-SiNPs to construct a probe for fluorescence turn-off sensing S2- in aqueous solution due to the IFE. In the detection system, a good linearity with S2- concentration in the range of 0–50 μM was accomplished. And as low as 0.1 μM S2- could be successfully detected. Moreover, the F-SiNPs displayed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, was further utilized for cell imaging. These results demonstrated the promising applications of F-SiNPs in S2- analysis and bioimaging.

Chao Yang: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ting Li: Investigation, Data curation. Qin Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Yongming Guo: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Tao Tao: Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by National College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (202110300010), Jiangsu Province College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (202110300014Z), College Students' Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of NUIST (XJDC202110300158), the Undergraduate Excellent Graduation Designing (Thesis) Supporting Program of Nanjing University of Information, Science and Techbology, the Foundation of NUIST-UoR International Research Institute, the Startup Foundation for Introducing Talent of NUIST (2018r056), the Jiangsu Province Natural Science Foundation (No. BK20201389), Qing Lan Project of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (No. R2018Q03). We are very grateful to Professor Zhuo Wang of Beijing University of Chemical Technology for her kind help.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121048.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared fluorescent nanomaterials for bioimaging and sensing
Adv. Opt. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Reineck, B.C. Gibson, Near-infrared fluorescent nanomaterials for bioimaging and sensing, Advanced Optical Materials 5 (2017) 1600446.
